data.description:
# Asymmetric attestation foundation — DSSE envelope + SSHSIG scheme + pluggable-scheme substrate

## Context
rebar signs its attestations (plan-review, completion-verifier verdicts) with a **symmetric HMAC**
keyed on a per-environment secret (`src/rebar/signing.py`; `.signing-key` / `REBAR_SIGNING_KEY`).
Two upcoming capabilities — per-person **authorship** signing (the identity epic) and
third-party-verifiable **operation certificates** (the op-cert epic) — both need **asymmetric**
signatures (verify-by-anyone, forge-by-none) over attestation bytes, verified offline. Building
that signing layer twice would duplicate security-critical code and worsen the namespace collision
already forming on the `SIGNATURE` event type / `signature` / `key_id` fields. **Beneficiary:** the
two consumer epics (and their end users) — they get one hardened, standards-based signing substrate
instead of two divergent stacks.

## Approach (and why)
Reuse established standards rather than hand-roll crypto:
- **DSSE (Dead Simple Signing Envelope)** as the envelope, signing `PAE(payloadType, body)` over
  exact opaque bytes. PAE eliminates the JSON re-serialization / canonicalization attack class (we
  never re-serialize parsed JSON to verify) and carries **no in-band `alg`** the verifier trusts —
  closing the JWT `alg`-confusion class (RFC 8725). DSSE is the Sigstore/in-toto/SLSA standard;
  ~30 lines, offline, scheme-agnostic.
- **SSHSIG via OpenSSH `ssh-keygen -Y`** (stdlib `subprocess`, **no new core dependency**) as the
  first asymmetric scheme — reuses the reference implementation and developers' existing SSH keys;
  verified offline against an `allowed_signers`-format trust input. Fail-closed was confirmed
  experimentally (wrong namespace / unknown principal / substituted key / tampered bytes / expired
  window all exit non-zero; `-Y check-novalidate` is structure-only and is never used for authz).
- **Pluggable scheme registry + per-kind policy table** (`kind → {scheme, namespace, trust-root}`),
  pinned out-of-band; the verifier **never** selects the scheme from record data. The existing
  **HMAC is registered as a legacy scheme** so current attestations keep verifying unchanged (this
  is the expand phase; the HMAC→asymmetric cutover for operation-certs is the op-cert epic's
  contract phase).

Alternative considered and rejected: `securesystemslib` for signing — it has **no SSH signer** (so
it would not cover the authorship path) and pulls `cryptography` (heavy) for Ed25519; core stays
stdlib-only.

## Scope
- **In:** a new attestation-substrate module (DSSE envelope + PAE; scheme registry; SSHSIG scheme
  via subprocess; per-kind policy table; verify-dispatch with domain separation); registration of
  the existing HMAC as a legacy scheme; an ADR; unit/behavioral tests.
- **Out:** any identity entity or authorship kind (identity epic); any change to operation-cert
  behavior / actor / enforcement (op-cert epic); **removing** HMAC (op-cert epic's contract phase).

## Success Criteria
- One signing substrate exists that both consumer epics import; no consumer needs its own crypto.
- Signatures are produced/verified over DSSE-PAE bytes; verification never re-serializes parsed JSON
  and never trusts an in-band algorithm.
- The SSHSIG scheme signs/verifies offline via `ssh-keygen` with **no new core runtime dependency**
  and fails closed on every bad input.
- Existing plan-review / completion-verifier attestations still verify (HMAC as a registered legacy
  scheme) — no behavior regression.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A new module (e.g. `src/rebar/attest/`) exposes `sign(kind, body_bytes) -> envelope` and
      `verify(kind, envelope, trust_root) -> verdict`, where `kind` selects `{scheme, namespace,
      trust-root}` from an out-of-band policy table (not from the envelope). A unit test asserts a
      record whose in-band scheme field is tampered still verifies under the policy-pinned scheme
      (no `alg` confusion).
- [ ] DSSE PAE is implemented per the v1.0.0 spec
      (`PAE(type,body)="DSSEv1" SP len(type) SP type SP len(body) SP body`), proven by a unit test
      against the spec's worked example; a test with duplicate/re-ordered JSON keys demonstrates
      verify operates on the exact stored bytes and does not re-serialize.
- [ ] The SSHSIG scheme signs and verifies via `ssh-keygen -Y` in a subprocess using argv arrays
      (no shell), branches only on exit code, and allowlist-validates namespace + principal. A test
      matrix asserts fail-closed on: tampered bytes, wrong namespace, unknown principal, substituted
      key, and expired validity window.
- [ ] The existing HMAC signer is registered as a legacy scheme; a plan-review/completion-verifier
      attestation created before this change still returns verdict `certified` (regression test).
- [ ] `ssh-keygen` availability/version (>= 8.9 for validity intervals) is detected; its absence
      fails closed with a clear error (test asserts no silent pass).
- [ ] An ADR under `docs/adr/` records the substrate: DSSE envelope, pluggable scheme, per-kind
      policy table, domain separation, HMAC-as-legacy.

## Intended decomposition (children)
1. DSSE envelope + PAE module (+ spec-example + duplicate-key tests).
2. Scheme registry + per-kind policy table + verify-dispatch (domain separation; alg-confusion test).
3. SSHSIG scheme via `ssh-keygen` (fail-closed subprocess contract + test matrix).
4. Register existing HMAC as a legacy scheme (regression test).
5. ADR + docs.

## Prior art / grounding
DSSE protocol v1.0.0 (secure-systems-lab/dsse); in-toto attestation; OpenSSH PROTOCOL.sshsig +
`allowed_signers`; git SSH commit signing (`gpg.ssh.allowedSignersFile`); RFC 8725 (JWT BCP);
RFC 8785 (JCS, considered and rejected in favour of DSSE PAE). Fail-closed behavior of
`ssh-keygen -Y verify` confirmed by direct experiment in this environment (OpenSSH 10.2).
data.title: Asymmetric attestation foundation — DSSE envelope + SSHSIG scheme + pluggable-scheme substrate
